items.name: Cup of Tea (cafe, builder's)
items.fact: A builder's tea cost 30p in 1990 and has risen to £1.80 today, a 6x increase that still makes it one of the cheapest hot drinks in the country. The UK drinks 100 million cups of tea every single day.

items.name: First-Class Stamp
items.fact: The first-class stamp shot from 22p in 1990 to £1.35 in 2025, a 514% rise that is six times general inflation. Royal Mail justified the hike by pointing to a 70% drop in letter volumes since 2004.

items.name: Freddo Chocolate Frog
items.fact: The Freddo rose from 5p to 30p in 35 years, a 500% increase that became a genuine national controversy. MPs debated it in Parliament, and economists now use the Freddo as an unofficial but beloved inflation benchmark.

items.name: Mars Bar (standard, 51g)
items.fact: Mars was created in Slough in 1932 and cost 22p in 1990. The bar quietly shrank from 65g to 51g between 2010 and 2017 while the price rose, a move that sparked the UK's most widely covered 'shrinkflation' outrage.

items.name: Pint of Milk (semi-skimmed, 568ml)
items.fact: Milk cost 28p a pint in 1990 and famously dipped below the cost of bottled water for much of the 2010s, forcing thousands of British dairy farmers out of business as supermarkets used it as a loss leader.

items.name: Bag of Crisps (Walkers Ready Salted, standard)
items.fact: Walkers crisps now contain roughly 30% fewer crisps per bag than in the 1990s due to 'slack fill' practices. Gary Lineker has promoted Walkers since 1994, making him one of the longest-running brand ambassadors in British advertising history.

items.name: Newspaper (Daily Mirror or The Sun)
items.fact: The Sun was sold for 20p in 1990 and crossed £1 for the first time in 2023. Its circulation fell from 4 million daily readers in 1990 to under 1 million by 2025, yet the cover price rose 6x as publishers charged more to shrinking audiences.

items.name: Can of Coca-Cola (330ml, corner shop)
items.fact: A can of Coke from a corner shop cost 33p in 1990. The Sugar Tax introduced in 2018 added 24p per litre to full-sugar drinks, and Coca-Cola's UK price passed £1 for the first time that same year.

items.name: Loaf of Sliced White Bread (Hovis/Kingsmill)
items.fact: A Hovis loaf cost 49p in 1990. The 2022 wheat price spike caused by Russia's Ukraine invasion pushed bread prices up 20% in under a year, the fastest single-year rise since the 1970s.

items.name: Penny Chews (Black Jacks / Fruit Salads, each)
items.fact: Black Jacks and Fruit Salads were literally a penny each in 1990, and the concept of 'penny sweets' died as they crossed 5p then 10p. The corner shop pick-and-mix tradition is one of the most mourned casualties of British high street inflation.

items.name: Bottle of Brown Sauce (HP, 255g)
items.fact: HP Sauce was named after the Houses of Parliament and has been a British breakfast staple since 1895. Its production was controversially moved from Birmingham to the Netherlands in 2007, causing national outrage despite the recipe remaining identical.

items.name: Freddo Caramel (single)
items.fact: The Freddo Caramel has always sat a few pence above the original Freddo, and both have become symbols of confectionery inflation. A 2023 survey found 78% of Brits cited Freddo price rises as their most visceral awareness of inflation.

items.name: Bus Single Fare (city, short journey)
items.fact: Bus fares were capped at £2 per single journey across England in January 2023, a rare government intervention. The cap prevented fares from rising toward £3 or £4 in some areas and was extended several times due to its popularity.

items.name: Box of 10 Teabags (PG Tips / Yorkshire)
items.fact: PG Tips was the UK's best-selling tea brand from the 1950s until 2019, when Yorkshire Tea overtook it. The PG Tips chimpanzee adverts ran for 45 years and were voted Britain's most popular TV advert campaign of all time.

items.name: Biro Pen (single, Bic Cristal)
items.fact: The Bic Cristal has been made to an almost identical design since 1950 and is the world's best-selling pen with over 100 billion sold. It was selected by the Museum of Modern Art in New York as a design object in its permanent collection.

items.name: Cadbury Crunchie Bar
items.fact: Cadbury was bought by Mondelez (formerly Kraft) in 2010 for £11.5 billion in one of Britain's most controversial corporate takeovers. The acquisition prompted Parliamentary hearings after Cadbury's Somerdale factory was closed despite takeover assurances.

items.name: McDonalds Cheeseburger
items.fact: The McDonald's cheeseburger held at 99p for nearly a decade before crossing £1 in 2023, a moment widely covered as a symbolic end of an era in British fast food value. McDonald's UK blamed energy and ingredient cost increases.

items.name: Single Egg (free-range, large)
items.fact: Free-range egg prices doubled between 2021 and 2023 due to avian flu wiping out millions of birds. The UK culled over 50 million birds in 2022 alone, the worst outbreak in British history, causing supermarket egg shortages for the first time in decades.

items.name: Pint of Bitter (pub, regional average)
items.fact: The average pint crossed £5 in 2023 for the first time in British history. Over 7,000 pubs closed between 2015 and 2025 as rising costs outpaced what drinkers would pay, ending a pub culture that had persisted since the 13th century.

items.name: Packet of Ibuprofen (16 tablets, generic)
items.fact: Generic ibuprofen is one of Britain's most price-stable consumer products, rising barely 120% in 35 years against 300% general inflation. NHS bulk purchasing agreements create a price ceiling that keeps pharmacy shelves competitively priced.

items.name: Postcard (seaside souvenir shop)
items.fact: Britain invented the picture postcard and was the first country to run postcard-sending as a mass hobby in the 1900s. The saucy seaside postcard, synonymous with Blackpool, is now a collectors item as seaside shops struggle to sell them at all.

items.name: Tube of Toothpaste (Colgate, standard 75ml)
items.fact: Colgate controls roughly half the UK toothpaste market. UK supermarkets started selling own-brand toothpaste for 29p in the 2010s, directly challenging Colgate and forcing it to justify its price premium through whitening and sensitivity variants.

items.name: Tin of Heinz Baked Beans (415g)
items.fact: Heinz Baked Beans are eaten by 1.5 million British households every day and are considered a cultural institution. In 2023 Heinz raised prices so aggressively that Tesco removed its entire range from shelves, triggering a supermarket standoff covered live on BBC News.

items.name: Pork Sausage (single, butcher's)
items.fact: British sausages contain a minimum 42% pork by law, a standard that became a Brexit flashpoint as the UK refused to lower it to match some EU standards. The 'Great British Banger' became a symbol of post-Brexit food sovereignty.

items.name: Ice Lolly (Fab or Calippo, corner shop)
items.fact: The Fab lolly has been made to the same recipe since 1967 and is a British summer institution. A viral 2023 petition demanded the government freeze ice lolly prices after the Fab crossed £1 for the first time.

items.name: Cinema Ticket (Odeon/Vue, standard 2D)
items.fact: A UK cinema ticket cost £3 in 1990 and has risen 5x by 2025. Odeon, Vue and Cineworld all introduced monthly subscription passes in the 2010s to fight Netflix, but Cineworld went bankrupt in 2022 despite having 9 million subscribers.

items.name: Paperback Novel (Penguin, standard)
items.fact: Penguin Books launched in 1935 selling books for sixpence to match the price of a packet of cigarettes, deliberately democratising reading. A Penguin paperback now costs £12.99, roughly the price of two pints in a London pub.

items.name: Pint of Lager (London pub, premium)
items.fact: A pint of premium lager in a London pub crossed £7 in 2023, more than double the national average and prompting widespread disbelief. London now has the second most expensive beer prices of any major city in the world, behind only Oslo.

items.name: Men's Haircut (high street barber)
items.fact: A British barber haircut rose from £4 to £20 in 35 years. Turkish barbers, which proliferated in UK high streets in the 2010s, briefly kept prices lower with a full-service cut and wet shave for £10, before normalising toward the market rate.

items.name: Fish and Chips (takeaway, standard portion)
items.fact: Fish and chips cost £1.50 in 1990 and crossed £10 in 2023 due to energy price spikes for deep fat fryers and cod price hikes. Over 3,000 chip shops closed between 2020 and 2025, roughly one in ten, threatening a 150-year national institution.

items.name: Meal Deal (supermarket, sandwich plus drink plus snack)
items.fact: The supermarket meal deal is possibly the most studied item in British retail economics. Tesco held its deal at £3 for years before inflation forced it to £3.90 in 2023, prompting more press coverage than most Cabinet reshuffles.

items.name: Tube Single Journey (London, Zone 1)
items.fact: The London Tube single fare for Zone 1 rose from 80p to £3.70 in 35 years. The Oyster card launched in 2003 introduced cheaper capped fares and removed the incentive to buy paper tickets, reshaping how millions of Londoners interact with money.

items.name: Large Domino's Pizza (Margherita, collection)
items.fact: Domino's UK entered the market in 1985 and grew to 1,200 stores. It almost always runs promotional codes discounting pizzas by 40-50%, meaning very few customers pay the headline price, making the listed price largely a fictional anchor.

items.name: Monthly Gym Membership (mid-range chain)
items.fact: PureGym launched in 2009 offering memberships from £9.99 with no contract, cutting average UK gym prices by 30%. It forced traditional chains to drop prices and killed many of them, reshaping UK fitness as a low-margin volume business.

items.name: Greggs Sausage Roll plus Drink Combo
items.fact: Greggs serves 8 million customers per week and has more UK stores than McDonald's. The sausage roll and drink deal emerged as one of Britain's most popular budget lunch options after food prices rose sharply from 2022 onward.

items.name: National Express Coach (London to Manchester)
items.fact: National Express London to Manchester tickets can be bought for £5 in advance, making it cheaper than a Zone 1 Tube return. The coach undercuts train prices so dramatically that it is the transport choice of students, low-wage workers and the environmentally conscious alike.

items.name: Monthly Netflix Subscription (standard HD)
items.fact: Netflix UK launched in 2012 at £5.99 a month and trebled in price by 2025. In the same period the BBC licence fee, which funds 8 TV channels and 10 radio networks, rose from £145.50 to £174.50 annually, or just £14.54 per month.

items.name: Premier League Match Ticket (average away)
items.fact: The Premier League capped away ticket prices at £30 from 2016 to 2023 following fan protests. Before the cap some away tickets cost £90+, and Manchester United had the most expensive average ticket in world football at one point.

items.name: Pack of 20 Cigarettes (Marlboro Gold)
items.fact: UK cigarette prices rose 8x since 1990, driven by escalating tobacco duty that now accounts for 80% of the retail price. England became the first country in the world to pass a generational smoking ban in 2024, prohibiting anyone born after 2009 from ever legally buying cigarettes.

items.name: Curry House Curry (chicken tikka masala, restaurant)
items.fact: Chicken tikka masala was declared Britain's national dish by Foreign Secretary Robin Cook in 2001. Post-Brexit immigration restrictions devastated UK curry houses as they rely almost entirely on chefs from Bangladesh and India, and over 1,000 closed between 2016 and 2024.

items.name: Monthly Spotify Subscription (premium individual)
items.fact: Spotify launched in the UK in 2008 and held its £9.99 price for over a decade. The UK music industry collapsed from £1.3 billion in 1999 to £600 million in 2014 as streaming replaced purchases, before recovering to £1.5 billion by 2023 entirely through subscription growth.

items.name: Pint of Guinness (London pub)
items.fact: Guinness sales in the UK rose 25% between 2020 and 2024, defying the overall pub decline. A global shortage of Guinness in 2024 was caused by demand outstripping supply at the Dublin brewery for the first time in the brand 265-year history.

items.name: Primark Basic Hoodie (adult)
items.fact: Primark hoodies briefly became more expensive than their T-shirts as cotton prices spiked in 2022. Primark remains one of the only major fashion retailers without an online store, insisting customers must visit physically to encourage impulse purchases.

items.name: Return Train Ticket (London to Brighton, off-peak)
items.fact: The London to Brighton off-peak return has risen 4x since 1990. When privatisation happened in 1996, rail fares were promised to fall in real terms. Instead they rose faster than wages, making British rail fares among the highest in Europe per kilometre.

items.name: Bottle of Kopparberg Cider (500ml, shop)
items.fact: Kopparberg pioneered the fruit cider category in the UK after entering from Sweden in 2007. By 2023 fruit cider outsold traditional cider in UK supermarkets, turning a niche Scandinavian product into a cornerstone of British summer drinking.

items.name: Cadbury Selection Box (small, Christmas)
items.fact: Cadbury's Christmas Selection Box has been a British festive institution since 1933. A 2023 investigation found the box's total chocolate weight had shrunk by 40% over 20 years, making it the most well-documented example of British confectionery shrinkflation.

items.name: Adult Day Return (London Overground, avg zone)
items.fact: London Overground fares rise by the RPI inflation figure every January, a system that has compounded above wage growth for over a decade. A season ticket from outer London to central London now costs over £5,000 per year.

items.name: Greggs Festive Bake (seasonal, single)
items.fact: The Greggs Festive Bake launches every November and generates more social media coverage than most product launches. In 2022 a Festive Bake shortage caused actual public complaints to Greggs headquarters, marking the first time a pasty triggered a customer service crisis.

items.name: BBC TV Licence (annual, colour)
items.fact: The BBC licence fee was frozen from 2010 to 2017 and again from 2022 to 2024 under government pressure, but the BBC argued that each freeze cost it hundreds of millions in real-terms cuts. Its abolition has been debated at every election since 2019.

items.name: Afternoon Tea (Bettys or mid-range hotel)
items.fact: Afternoon tea at Bettys in Harrogate has had a waiting list since the 1980s. The Ritz London charges £85 for afternoon tea in 2025, turning a Victorian middle-class custom into one of London's most expensive everyday cultural experiences.

items.name: Nike Air Max 90 (standard colourway)
items.fact: The Nike Air Max 90 launched in 1990 at £55 and became the defining British street shoe of the decade. Nike UK limited edition drops now generate queues of hundreds outside Carnaby Street stores, with some pairs reselling for £500 on StockX within hours.

items.name: Monthly Rail Season Ticket (London commuter, avg)
items.fact: Monthly rail season ticket prices have doubled in real terms since privatisation in 1994. An annual season ticket from Reading to London now costs over £4,500, more than workers in many UK regions earn in two months of take-home pay.

items.name: Dyson Cordless Vacuum (entry model)
items.fact: James Dyson was rejected by 5,126 manufacturers before bringing his bagless vacuum to market in 1993. Dyson now holds over 7,000 patents and generates £2 billion in annual revenue, making it the UK's most successful engineering export of the last 30 years.

items.name: Glastonbury Weekend Ticket
items.fact: Glastonbury tickets cost £1 in 1970 and £38 in 1990. They now sell out in under 30 minutes despite costing £375 plus booking fees. The 2023 festival generated an estimated £400 million for the local Somerset economy.

items.name: Economy Flight (London to Edinburgh, avg)
items.fact: EasyJet launched in 1995 with £29 London to Edinburgh fares, famously undercutting British Airways by 80% and forcing every UK carrier to adopt dynamic pricing. Post-COVID fare spikes reversed decades of falling prices as airlines cut capacity.

items.name: Le Creuset Casserole Dish (24cm, cast iron)
items.fact: Le Creuset casserole dishes are passed down in wills in the UK more than almost any other household item. The brand has a 30-year guarantee because the pots genuinely last generations, making them one of the few cookware items that appreciates as an heirloom.

items.name: Hunter Wellies (original, full length)
items.fact: Hunter wellies have been made in Scotland since 1856 and supplied to the British Army in World War One. Kate Middleton wearing them at Glastonbury in 2005 turned them from farm footwear into a luxury fashion item, tripling sales within two years.

items.name: iPad (Apple base model, 10.9-inch)
items.fact: The first iPad launched in the UK at £429 in 2010. Despite a decade of inflation, the base iPad costs less in nominal pounds today than at launch, one of the few consumer electronics products in Britain that has fallen in real-terms price over 15 years.

items.name: Annual English Heritage Membership (individual)
items.fact: English Heritage membership gives access to over 400 historic sites including Stonehenge. At £67 per year it grants more than £300 of free entry, making it widely cited as the best-value annual subscription in Britain.

items.name: Annual Car Insurance (avg, fully comprehensive)
items.fact: UK car insurance premiums rose 58% in 2023 alone, the largest annual jump on record. Insurers blamed EV batteries costing £15,000 to replace after minor accidents, rising fraud claims, and courts awarding larger personal injury payouts.

items.name: Brompton Folding Bicycle (entry, M2L)
items.fact: Brompton bikes are handmade in west London and have been manufactured there since 1975. Every Brompton takes 4.5 hours to build by hand and the company has a waiting list of up to 12 months, making it the only mass-market British product with a luxury heritage waiting list.

items.name: KitchenAid Stand Mixer (Artisan, base colour)
items.fact: The KitchenAid stand mixer design has been virtually unchanged since 1937. It became a Great British Bake Off status symbol after 2010 and UK sales tripled in the years following the show's launch, making it the only kitchen appliance with a cultural moment.

items.name: Tatler Subscription (annual, print plus digital)
items.fact: Tatler is Britain's oldest magazine, founded in 1709 by Richard Steele. Its 2021 cover featuring the Duchess of Cambridge as a working mother caused a Palace briefing against its own favoured publication, the only time a magazine caused a royal diplomatic incident.

items.name: Barbour Waxed Jacket (Beaufort style, basic)
items.fact: Barbour jackets are made in South Shields and have been worn by the royal family for generations. They require re-waxing every few years and Barbour offers a lifetime repair service, making them one of the few British fashion items that are genuinely repairable.

items.name: Weekend Hotel Stay (mid-range, London, per night)
items.fact: London hotel prices hit record highs in 2023 and 2024 as post-COVID demand collided with reduced supply. A mid-range 3-star hotel in central London now averages £200 per night, pushing many British families to choose seaside caravans over city breaks.

items.name: Kindle E-Reader (base model, Amazon UK)
items.fact: The Kindle launched in the UK in 2009 and immediately became the fastest-selling Amazon product in British history. UK ebook sales crossed physical book sales for the first time in 2012, then physical books made a comeback after 2016, confounding every publishing industry prediction.

items.name: Bluetooth Speaker (JBL Flip, mid-range)
items.fact: Portable Bluetooth speakers have barely changed in price since 2015 despite general inflation of over 35%. Chinese competitors flooded the UK market with £25 alternatives, forcing brands like JBL to prove quality through durability and add waterproofing to justify their price.

items.name: Annual AA Membership (basic roadside cover)
items.fact: The AA was founded in 1905 to help motorists evade police speed traps, which is why AA patrol men once saluted members: not saluting meant a speed trap was ahead. It now has 17 million members and handles 10,000 breakdowns per day.

items.name: Wickes Kitchen Tap (mid-range chrome mixer)
items.fact: Mixer taps were a relative luxury in British kitchens until the 1990s, when most homes still had separate hot and cold taps. The separate tap design persisted in the UK long after Europe had universally adopted mixer taps, baffling every foreign visitor.

items.name: Wetherspoons Full English Breakfast
items.fact: Wetherspoons launched its cheap full English in the mid-1990s and for decades offered it for under £4, becoming a British institution for budget dining. When it crossed £7 in 2023, it became a widely shared metric that even cheap Britain had ended.

items.name: Peloton Bike (hardware, UK price)
items.fact: Peloton entered the UK in 2018 and briefly had a 6-month delivery waiting list during lockdown. The company cut the UK bike price three times between 2022 and 2025 as demand collapsed, and its London Covent Garden showroom became a symbol of pandemic-era overreach.

items.name: Wimbledon Ground Admission (daily queue ticket)
items.fact: Wimbledon's overnight queue is one of Britain's most famous social institutions and the only major sporting event in the world where thousands of people voluntarily camp outside. The All England Club distributes a 37-page Queue Guide with etiquette rules including a ban on saving spaces.

items.name: Prescription Charge (NHS, per item, England)
items.fact: England is the only UK nation that still charges for NHS prescriptions. Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland abolished charges between 2007 and 2010. The English prescription charge has risen 225% since 1990, and a prepayment certificate at £111 per year covers unlimited items.

items.name: M25 Toll (Dartford Crossing, car, peak)
items.fact: The Dartford Crossing was originally meant to become toll-free once construction costs were repaid in 2002. Instead tolls were kept permanently under a different name and raised regularly, becoming one of the most cited examples of government mission creep in UK transport policy.

items.name: Chelsea vs Arsenal Match Ticket (Category B, home end)
items.fact: A top Premier League match cost £8 in 1990. The formation of the Premier League in 1992 triggered a sustained 30-year ticket price spiral. Arsenal's Emirates Stadium, opened in 2006, was at the time the most expensive place to watch football in the world.

items.name: iPhone 16 Pro (128GB, UK price)
items.fact: The first iPhone sold in the UK in 2007 exclusively through O2 on contract. Apple launched the iPhone 16 Pro at £1,199 in 2024, and the UK price is typically £100 to £200 higher than the US equivalent after import duties and VAT.

items.name: New VW Golf (base, 1.0 TSI Life)
items.fact: The VW Golf has been Britain's best-selling car for much of the last 40 years. Its base price has more than trebled since 1990, and the electric ID.3 replacement was launched at £35,000, putting the Golf nameplate firmly out of reach for first-time buyers.

items.name: London Zone 1-6 Annual Travelcard
items.fact: A Zone 1-6 annual Travelcard rose from £620 to £3,372 in 35 years, consuming an increasing share of London wages. TfL's 2016 introduction of pay-as-you-go daily caps partially rendered annual cards obsolete for hybrid workers.

items.name: MacBook Air (M2, base UK price)
items.fact: The MacBook Air launched in 2008 at £1,299 and is now priced within £1 of its launch price in nominal terms, a 15-year standstill while performance increased 50-fold. It regularly tops UK Which? and consumer satisfaction surveys as the best laptop under £1,500.

items.name: Annual University Tuition (UK student, England)
items.fact: English university tuition went from free in 1997 to £9,250 in 2017, where it has been frozen ever since. Total UK student debt crossed £236 billion in 2024, the highest proportion of GDP of any country in the world that uses an income-contingent repayment model.

items.name: Burberry Trench Coat (Westminster, classic beige)
items.fact: The Burberry trench coat was invented for WWI officers and has since become Britain's most copied fashion item. Burberry destroyed £28 million of unsold stock in 2018 rather than discount it, causing a national scandal that prompted new laws on fashion waste.

items.name: London Zone 2 Studio Flat (monthly rent, avg)
items.fact: A Zone 2 studio flat in London cost £350 per month in 1990 and averages £2,200 by 2025, a 6x rise against wages that roughly doubled. London renters now spend an average 46% of take-home pay on rent, compared to 18% in 1990.

items.name: Rolls-Royce Ghost (base MSRP, UK)
items.fact: Rolls-Royce is manufactured in Goodwood, West Sussex and has record global sales every year since 2020. The Ghost is now its entry-level model, as Russian oligarch and Chinese billionaire demand pushed the average Rolls-Royce transaction price to over £400,000.

items.name: Gold (troy ounce, London spot price, GBP)
items.fact: The UK gold price is set twice daily in London at the famous LBMA fix, a process unchanged since 1919. Gordon Brown sold 60% of Britain's gold reserves between 1999 and 2002 at prices near historic lows, a decision that cost the Treasury an estimated £7 billion.

items.name: Average UK House Price (England)
items.fact: The average English house cost £58,000 in 1990 and £295,000 in 2025, a 5x rise against wages that roughly doubled. The ratio of house prices to average wages in London reached 12:1 by 2025, the highest since records began.

items.name: Wimbledon Centre Court Debenture Seat (5-year)
items.fact: Wimbledon debenture seats sell for £2,500 to £5,000 per 5-year block on the official market. On resale platforms individual match tickets for finals days fetch over £2,000, making them the most expensive sporting tickets in Britain.

items.name: Patek Philippe Aquanaut (entry, ref 5167A)
items.fact: Patek Philippe makes fewer than 70,000 watches per year deliberately and never discounts. Their famous slogan is 'You never actually own a Patek Philippe, you merely look after it for the next generation,' a line so effective it is quoted as genuine financial wisdom.

items.name: Private School Fees (annual, boarding, top public school)
items.fact: Eton annual fees reached £55,000 in 2025. The UK government added 20% VAT to private school fees in January 2025, forcing many schools to raise fees by up to 25% overnight in the largest single shock to private education pricing in a generation.

items.name: Claridge's Hotel 2-Night Stay (standard room)
items.fact: Claridge's has been called the annex of Buckingham Palace and rooms now start at £750 per night. The hotel's Art Deco interiors are the most photographed hotel interior in Britain, and its lobby Christmas tree has been designed by Dior, Chanel and John Galliano.

items.name: Tesla Model 3 (standard range, UK price)
items.fact: Tesla launched the Model 3 in the UK at £44,000 in 2019. Despite cuts the UK price remains £3,000 to £5,000 above the equivalent US price due to import tariffs. The UK government scrapped its EV purchase grant of up to £1,500 in 2022, adding directly to consumer cost.

items.name: Fortnum and Mason Grand Hamper (ultra-luxury tier)
items.fact: Fortnum and Mason has been making hampers since 1738 and supplied provisions to Wellington's army at Waterloo. Its top hamper is gifted to every incoming head of state visiting Britain and is considered Britain's most diplomatically useful food product.

items.name: Harley Street Consultant (specialist, annual package)
items.fact: Harley Street specialist annual health packages start at £2,500 in 2025, covering full MOT checks with MRI, blood panels and consultations. What NHS patients sometimes wait 18 months to receive is available on Harley Street within 48 hours.

items.name: Chelsea FC Season Ticket (Matthew Harding Lower)
items.fact: A Chelsea season ticket cost £120 in 1990 and rose 13x by 2025. The Premier League's transformation from a working-class game to a global entertainment product priced out the original fan base: the average Premier League fan is now 46 years old.

items.name: UK Kitchen Renovation (mid-range, full refit)
items.fact: Kitchen renovation costs rose 60% in the five years to 2025, driven by the 2021 timber shortage and a post-COVID construction labour gap of over 200,000 workers. A NEFF or Siemens induction hob alone now costs what a full fitted kitchen cost in 1995.

items.name: Return Business Class Flight (London to New York)
items.fact: Concorde offered a London to New York business class crossing in 3.5 hours at £6,000 return before it was retired in 2003. Nothing has replaced it, and the three-fold speed reduction means a business class passenger now spends 7 hours more crossing the Atlantic than their 1990s predecessor.

items.name: Rolex Submariner Date (new, UK retail)
items.fact: The Rolex Submariner's UK retail price rose from £2,200 to £9,300 in 35 years. On the grey market it sells for £12,000 to £15,000 because Rolex limits production artificially, making watches purchased at retail immediately worth more than you paid.

items.name: New Build One-Bedroom Flat (London Help to Buy avg)
items.fact: A new-build one-bedroom flat in London cost £60,000 in 1990 and averages £480,000 in 2025. A first-time buyer on an average London salary of £40,000 would need to save for 12 years to afford a 10% deposit, up from 3 years in 1990.

items.name: Grand National Hospitality Package (Aintree)
items.fact: The Grand National is watched by 600 million people globally and is Britain's most bet-on single sporting event. Corporate hospitality packages have risen 19x since 1990 as the race transformed from a sporting event into a national fashion and entertainment spectacle.

items.name: Jaguar F-Type (base convertible, UK MSRP)
items.fact: The Jaguar F-Type launched in 2013 at £57,600, replacing the XK. Jaguar Land Rover announced in 2024 that Jaguar would go all-electric and completely rebrand, ending 86 years of the classic Jaguar identity and sparking the most divisive brand debate in British automotive history.

items.name: Harrods Luxury Gift Hamper (top tier)
items.fact: Harrods top-tier hamper now costs £2,800 and was bought by Mohamed Al Fayed in 1985 for £615 million, a price many considered extortionate at the time. He sold it to Qatar Holdings in 2010 for £1.5 billion, the biggest profit ever made on a British department store.
